---
title: "weekly temperature tracker"
format: html
---
Our project is a weekly temperature tracker program that records daily high and low temperatures for a seven-day period in Oklahoma City. The program stores these values in lists, calculates summary statistics (average, minimum, and maximum temperatures), and generates a line graph to visualize temperature trends. The graph also highlights the exact days on which the highest and lowest temperatures occur.

Goals: The goal of our program is to collect daily high and low temperature data for one week in Oklahoma City, compute summary statistics (mean, minimum, and maximum), and visualize the data using a graph. This allows us to observe patterns, compare temperature fluctuations, and identify extreme values throughout the week.

Data Resources: Temperature data was collected from the National Weather Service website. Daily high and low temperatures for the dates 4/6/26-4/12/26 in Oklahoma City were manually recorded and entered into the program as lists. The Apple Weather App also gives temperatures in Oklahoma City. 

Final Code:
```{python}
plt.figure()
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

days = ["Mon","Tue","Wed","Thu","Fri","Sat","Sun"]

lows = [42, 42, 44, 51, 58, 59, 65]
highs = [73, 71, 76, 80, 83, 77, 82]

# two lines only
plt.plot(days, highs, marker="o", label="Highs", color="red")
plt.plot(days, lows, marker="o", label="Lows", color="blue")

# combine for min/max
all_temps = highs + lows
all_days = days + days
all_values = highs + lows

min_temp = min(all_temps)
max_temp = max(all_temps)

min_index = all_values.index(min_temp)
max_index = all_values.index(max_temp)

# small highlights
plt.scatter(all_days[min_index], min_temp, s=200, color="black", label="Min Temp")
plt.scatter(all_days[max_index], max_temp, s=200, color="black", label="Max Temp")

plt.title("Weekly Temperatures for 4/6/26-4/12/26 in Oklahoma City")
plt.xlabel("Day")
plt.ylabel("Temperature (°F)")
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()

plt.show()
```

Code Explanation:
The lists 'highs' and 'lows' store the daily temperature values for the week. The lists are combined into 'all_temps' to determine the overall minimum and maximum temperatures across both datasets.
The 'min()' and 'max()' functions identify the lowest and highest values. The 'index()' function is used to locate the position of these values within the lists so the correct day can be identified and highlighted on the graph.
The 'days' list is used to label the x-axis so each temeprature value corresponds to the correct day of the week. 
The 'plt.plot()' function creates two lines that represent the high and low temperatures. The 'plt.scatter()' function highlights the minimum and maximum temperatures using larger markers to make them distinct from the rest of the temperatures. 
Labels, a title, a grid, and a legend are added to improve readability and clarity of the graph.

How to Run This Project:
To run this project, the user needs Python installed along with matplotlib library. The code can be executed in Positron, Jupyter Notebook, or any Python-compatible environment. 
1. Enter daily high and low temperatures into the 'highs' and 'lows' lists.
2. Run the code cells to calculate the minimum and maximim values.
3. Execute the plotting code to generate the graph. 
4. The graph will display temperature trends and highlight the highest and lowest values for the week.

Interpretation of Results: 
The graph shows how temperatures change throughout the week. The graph from our code shows a general upward trend in temperature over the course of the week. The highest temperature occurred later in the week. The highlighted points make it easy to identify the warmest and coolest days of the week.
This demonstrates how simple data analysis and visualization techniques can be used to identify patterns and trends in real-world weather data.
